access.entityLabel: General Corpus source XML for aew3422

entities.description: Exact XML artifact consumed by General Corpus and supplied through the pinned Kyvero-Press/CME submodule.
entities.label: General Corpus source XML for aew3422

rights.basis: The exact rights boundary between contributor-owned repository material and copied U-M content is not established; the repository license alone cannot relicense upstream material.
rights.component: Complete repository XML, including inherited U-M text and markup

rights.basis: The pinned CME repository asserts the Unlicense for software and source/binary forms. This is recorded only for contributor-owned original repository contributions, not as blanket permission for the complete XML or upstream content.
rights.component: Repository-contributed software-like wrapping and original contributions, if any

access.accessMethod: U-M CME item page
access.entityLabel: University of Michigan CME item AEW3422.0001.001
access.format: Web text and linked page images

entities.description: A 2006 Level 1 TEI encoding produced from uncorrected OCR with linked page images. It retains the carrier title page and encodes Plummer's work text on pp. 109-157; omitted front and back matter remain represented only by PB milestones.
entities.label: University of Michigan CME item AEW3422.0001.001
notes: Source description records 412 600-dpi TIFF G4 page images and no OCR corrections.

rights.basis: The current U-M item labels the item Public Domain and links the DPLA No Copyright - United States statement. This current assertion is preserved separately from the older XML-header warning.
The embedded header says copyright may apply where applicable and that other restrictions may govern distribution; it provides no item-specific open license.
notes: The discrepancy between this legacy generic statement and the current U-M item status is retained rather than silently reconciled.

entities.description: A twenty-chapter Middle English political and constitutional treatise addressed in its surviving form to royal governance, counsel, finance, and the contrast between regal and political dominion.
entities.label: Sir John Fortescue, The Governance of England

rights.basis: The work was composed in the fifteenth century and printed no later than 1714; this excludes modern photography, catalog text, encodings, and contractual access terms.

bibliographic.citation: Charles Plummer, ed., The Governance of England: Otherwise Called The Difference between an Absolute and a Limited Monarchy (Oxford: Clarendon Press, 1885), 387 pp.
bibliographic.title: The Governance of England: Otherwise Called The Difference between an Absolute and a Limited Monarchy

entities.description: Historically oriented edition in which the target occupies pp. 109-157. Laud Misc. 593 supplies the base through p. 155, line 27; Cotton Claudius A VIII supplies the remainder. Plummer collated ten witnesses and records selected variants.
entities.label: Charles Plummer's revised edition of The Governance of England (1885)

rights.basis: Published in 1885; the edition is in the public domain in the United States.

bibliographic.citation: John Fortescue-Aland, ed., The Difference between an Absolute and a Limited Monarchy (London: W. Bowyer, 1714).
bibliographic.title: The Difference between an Absolute and a Limited Monarchy

entities.description: First printed edition, based on Sir Adrian Fortescue's Digby 145 through Thomas Hearne's transcript and collated with Laud Misc. 593, Digby 198, and Cotton Claudius A VIII.
entities.label: John Fortescue-Aland's edition of The Difference between an Absolute and a Limited Monarchy (1714)

rights.basis: Published in 1714; the edition is in the public domain in the United States.

bibliographic.citation: Thomas Fortescue, Lord Clermont, ed., The Works of Sir John Fortescue, vol. 1 (London, 1869), pp. 445-474.
bibliographic.title: The Works of Sir John Fortescue

entities.description: Reprints the 1714 edition on pp. 449-474 after re-collation with Lambeth MS 262 and Harley MS 542; its introduction on pp. 445-447 documents the 1714 base and collations.
entities.label: Thomas Fortescue, Lord Clermont's edition in The Works of Sir John Fortescue, vol. 1 (1869)

rights.basis: Published in 1869; the edition is in the public domain in the United States.

access.accessMethod: Official collection catalog record
access.entityLabel: Bodleian Library, MS Laud Misc. 593

localCopies.label: Pinned Bodleian TEI catalog record for MS Laud Misc. 593
notes: Official TEI catalog metadata pinned to Bodleian medieval-mss commit cd36dce9; it describes the target but contains no manuscript page images or work transcription.

access.accessMethod: Bodleian Special Collections image and scan request service; quote shelfmark and target locus
access.entityLabel: Bodleian Library, MS Laud Misc. 593
notes: The same route applies to Digby 198 and Rawlinson B.384. No public complete facsimile for those three was verified on 2026-07-15.

entities.description: Plummer's principal base manuscript. His edition dates it circa 1480-1490; the current Bodleian catalog dates it 1500-1510. The copy is mutilated in Chapter XIX at the point represented on edition p. 155, line 27.
entities.label: Bodleian Library, MS Laud Misc. 593
notes: Plummer siglum L. Its chapter division is unique among the witnesses he collated.

rights.basis: Rights and reuse depend on the supplying institution's quotation, permission, and terms; catalog or request access does not grant publication rights.

access.entityLabel: British Library, Cotton MS Claudius A VIII

access.accessMethod: British Library physical-item request or reference-services enquiry; quote shelfmark and locus
access.entityLabel: British Library, Cotton MS Claudius A VIII
access.format: Physical consultation or reproduction on request
notes: The route also applies to Add MS 48031 A, Harley MS 542, and Harley MS 1757. No public complete facsimile of those four was verified on 2026-07-15; Harley 542's current record explicitly says its legacy digital images are unavailable.

dateStatements.label: Catalog date
dateStatements.value: Second half of the fifteenth century

entities.description: A late-fifteenth-century witness of Sir John Fortescue's De dominio regale et politico at current ff. 175-197. Plummer used it both in collation and to supply the ending missing from Laud Misc. 593.
entities.label: British Library, Cotton MS Claudius A VIII
notes: Plummer siglum C.

rights.basis: Rights and reuse depend on the supplying institution's quotation, permission, and terms; catalog or request access does not grant publication rights.

entities.description: Witness of the target at current ff. 148b-164, cataloged 1471-1476. Plummer describes a freely expanded and altered version, with the title Sir John Fortescue on the Governance of England.
entities.label: British Library, Add MS 48031 A (Yelverton MS 35)
notes: Plummer siglum Y; formerly Yelverton MS 35.

entities.description: Mid-sixteenth-century witness at ff. 196-203 that stops in Chapter XV because its scribe ceased copying, rather than through later physical mutilation.

holding.notes: The exact current BL catalog hierarchy and direct-record sequence checked on 2026-07-15 did not resolve a public item page; this is a dated search result, not a universal claim that no online record exists.

entities.label: British Library, Harley MS 1757
notes: Plummer siglum H2.

access.accessMethod: Official collection catalog record
access.entityLabel: Bodleian Library, MS Digby 145

localCopies.label: Pinned Bodleian TEI catalog record for MS Digby 145
notes: Official TEI catalog metadata pinned to Bodleian medieval-mss commit cd36dce9; it describes the target but contains no manuscript page images or work transcription.

entities.description: Complete witness copied by Sir Adrian Fortescue in 1532, target ff. 131v-159ar. A complete 372-canvas Digital Bodleian facsimile is public.
entities.label: Bodleian Library, MS Digby 145
notes: Plummer siglum D2.

access.accessMethod: Stemmatically inferred source; no shelfmark, holding institution, or extant object is identified.
access.entityLabel: Unidentified Digby 145-derived shared exemplar of Rawlinson B.384 and CUL Ii.3.11

entities.description: A lost or unidentified textual intermediary inferred by Plummer because Rawlinson B.384 and CUL Ii.3.11 repeatedly agree in departures from Digby 145 while each has lacunae absent from the other. Plummer concludes that both were copied from a text taken from Digby 145.
entities.label: Unidentified Digby 145-derived shared exemplar of Rawlinson B.384 and CUL Ii.3.11
notes: This is a stemmatically inferred intermediary, not a known surviving manuscript, and it must not be collapsed into Digby 145 itself.

access.accessMethod: Textually inferred source; no shelfmark, holding institution, or extant object is identified.
access.entityLabel: Unidentified Y-type source used by the CUL Ii.3.11 copyist

entities.description: An unidentified text of the Yelverton textual type inferred by Plummer from material and chapter titles entered in CUL Ii.3.11 in addition to its Digby-derived base.
entities.label: Unidentified Y-type source used by the CUL Ii.3.11 copyist
notes: This source is not equated with surviving Add MS 48031 A/Yelverton MS 35. Y-type denotes textual affinity only.

access.accessMethod: No present-location catalog record was established; begin with Bodleian Special Collections and Hearne diary references cited by Clermont.
access.entityLabel: Thomas Hearne's working transcript of Digby 145 (c. 1698-1699)
notes: Clermont identifies Hearne's diary volumes but does not establish that the separate working transcript survives there.

entities.description: A working transcript made by Thomas Hearne from Sir Adrian Fortescue's Digby 145 manuscript for John Fortescue-Aland, Lord Fortescue of Credan. Clermont quotes Hearne's diary statement that the 1714 edition was published from this copy without acknowledgment. Its present physical location and survival have not been verified.
entities.label: Thomas Hearne's working transcript of Digby 145 (c. 1698-1699)
notes: This is the documented intermediary behind the 1714 print, not the original Digby 145 witness and not a currently identified catalog object.

access.accessMethod: Digital Bodleian exact search followed by Special Collections enquiry
access.entityLabel: Bodleian Library, MS Rawlinson B.384
notes: No public complete facsimile was verified on 2026-07-15.

entities.description: Mid-seventeenth-century witness at ff. 42-68, copied in two hands and linguistically modernized. Plummer infers that it was copied from an unidentified shared text taken from Digby 145, not directly from Digby 145.

holding.notes: No exact current public catalog or Digital Bodleian object was verified on 2026-07-15.

entities.label: Bodleian Library, MS Rawlinson B.384
notes: Plummer siglum R.

rights.basis: Rights and reuse depend on the supplying institution's quotation, permission, and terms; catalog or request access does not grant publication rights.

access.accessMethod: CUL remote services and Special Collections image enquiry; quote MS Ii.3.11, ff. 214-241
access.entityLabel: Cambridge University Library, MS Ii.3.11
access.format: Scans or research assistance on request

entities.description: Seventeenth-century witness at ff. 214-241. Plummer infers that it and Rawlinson B.384 were copied from one unidentified text taken from Digby 145; its copyist additionally used a separate unidentified text of the Yelverton textual type.

holding.notes: The ArchivesSearch route was blocked by a WAF and no CUDL object was verified on 2026-07-15.

entities.label: Cambridge University Library, MS Ii.3.11
notes: Plummer siglum Cb; the shelfmark's first component is capital I plus lowercase i.

rights.basis: Rights and reuse depend on the supplying institution's quotation, permission, and terms; catalog or request access does not grant publication rights.

next_steps: Compare the full textual recensions and current Fortescue scholarship.
Retain 1471-1476 as the supported extant-work range meanwhile.
openQuestions.question: What is the exact composition, revision, and intended-recipient sequence between the Lancastrian exile context and the extant 1471-1476 form associated with Edward IV?

next_steps: Collate the endings and codicological transitions directly from reproductions.
Do not assert a stemmatic relation until that comparison is complete.
openQuestions.question: Do the Chapter-XVIII endings in Lambeth 262 and Digby 198 represent an earlier recension, deliberate abbreviation, or loss in a shared exemplar?

next_steps: Obtain images of Rawlinson B.384 and CUL Ii.3.11.
Perform passage-level collation and retain the relation as supported and explicitly mediated meanwhile.
openQuestions.question: What unidentified intermediary or intermediaries connect Digby 145 with Rawlinson B.384 and CUL Ii.3.11, and how did Yelverton-family readings enter the Cambridge text?

next_steps: Use the recorded institutional enquiry and imaging routes.
Request whole manuscripts when feasible and map target folios inside each full reproduction.
openQuestions.question: Can complete public or requestable digital facsimiles be obtained for the nine Plummer witnesses other than Digby 145?

next_steps: Seek item-specific written clarification from U-M.
Preserve both dated assertions until clarified.
openQuestions.question: How should the current U-M Public Domain / No Copyright - US label be reconciled with the generic legacy copyright-may-apply warning embedded in the 2006 XML?

reviewNotes: The abstract medieval work, its manuscript witnesses, three printed editions, U-M encoding, repository copy, and modern facsimiles are separate entities.

The XML's 412 PB milestones preserve the complete scanned carrier sequence, but only 50 BODY PB milestones correspond to the target's printed pp. 109-157 plus one leading blank; omitted front and back matter are page-image scaffolding, not transcribed work content.
The retained title page is zero-count carrier paratext and is not one of the work's twenty chapters.
Plummer's ten-witness collation is distinct from the two passage-scoped transcription bases.
The 1714 print branch is routed through Thomas Hearne's c. 1698-1699 working transcript; a direct 1714-to-Digby 145 transcription edge would flatten the documentary evidence.

Rawlinson B.384 and CUL Ii.3.11 are routed through Plummer's unidentified shared text taken from Digby 145. CUL's additional Y-type material is routed through a second unidentified source and is not equated with surviving Add MS 48031 A.

Seven exact official catalog deliverables are cached as metadata-only files: three pinned Bodleian TEI records, three British Library JSON records, and one Lambeth RDF/XML record. Each is marked target_work_presence absent because descriptive metadata is not a facsimile or transcription.

lineage.summary: The pinned General Corpus XML is a copy of University of Michigan CME item AEW3422.0001.001. Its immediate print source is Charles Plummer's revised 1885 edition, but the XML verbally encodes only the retained carrier title page and Plummer's target text on printed pages 109-157: twenty numbered chapters, not the edition's introduction, apparatus, notes, or back matter. Plummer used Bodleian MS Laud Misc. 593 as base through Chapter XIX at printed p. 155, line 27 and supplied the lost ending from British Library Cotton MS Claudius A VIII; he collated ten witnesses in all. Earlier editions of 1714 and 1869 belong to a separate print branch. Complete scans of the three editions and a complete 372-canvas facsimile of Digby 145 are cached; no public complete facsimile of the other nine witnesses was verified on 2026-07-15.

catalogSummary.author: Sir John Fortescue
genres: Advice to princes and governance counsel
catalogSummary.title: The Governance of England

catalogingSubject.label: The Governance of England
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The cataloged subject is Sir John Fortescue's twenty-chapter Middle English political treatise, composed or revised in the 1471-1476 period and represented by Charles Plummer's 1885 edition on printed pages 109-157. The record does not count the retained carrier title page, Plummer's introduction, critical apparatus, notes, or other omitted front and back matter as intrinsic work parts. The U-M XML's 412 PB milestones describe the full scanned carrier, while its verbal BODY encodes only the twenty chapters.

contentParts.label: The Governance of England
notes: The carrier title page is retained in the XML but excluded from this intrinsic work part.
contentParts.summary: Sir John Fortescue's twenty-chapter Middle English political and constitutional treatise.

contentParts.label: Chapter 1. The difference between dominium regale and dominium politicum et regale

locators.value: BODY DIV1 chapter 1; PB seq. 132-134; seq. 132 is a leading blank

notes: The leading blank PB milestone belongs to the carrier sequence; it is not an additional chapter or textual item.
contentParts.summary: Defines regal dominion and political-and-regal dominion.

contentParts.label: Chapter 2. Why one king reigns regally and another politically and regally
contentParts.summary: Explains why monarchies differ in constitutional form.

contentParts.label: Chapter 3. The fruits of jus regale and of jus politicum et regale
contentParts.summary: Compares the practical consequences of the two forms of rule.

contentParts.label: Chapter 4. How the revenues of France are made great
contentParts.summary: Examines French royal revenue and fiscal practice.

contentParts.label: Chapter 5. The harm that comes from a king's poverty
contentParts.summary: Argues that inadequate royal resources damage governance.

contentParts.label: Chapter 6. Ordinance for the king's ordinary charges
contentParts.summary: Discusses provision for recurring royal expenditure.

contentParts.label: Chapter 7. The king's extraordinary charges
contentParts.summary: Addresses exceptional royal expenditure.

contentParts.label: Chapter 8. If the king's livelihood does not suffice, his subjects ought to make it sufficient
contentParts.summary: Argues for adequate public provision for the Crown.

contentParts.label: Chapter 9. The perils that may come to the king from over-mighty subjects

locators.value: BODY DIV1 chapter 9; PB seq. 152-154; two P blocks

contentParts.summary: Warns against subjects whose power threatens royal government.

contentParts.label: Chapter 10. How the Crown is best to be endowed
contentParts.summary: Considers stable endowment of the Crown.

contentParts.label: Chapter 11. What of the king's livelihood given away may best be taken again
contentParts.summary: Discusses resumption of alienated royal revenues.

contentParts.label: Chapter 12. What harm would come to England if its commons were poor
contentParts.summary: Connects the prosperity of the commons with the strength of the realm.

contentParts.label: Chapter 13. Only lack of heart and cowardice keep the French from rising
contentParts.summary: Uses comparison with France to discuss political obedience and resistance.

contentParts.label: Chapter 14. Why a resumption and a grant of goods to the king are needed
contentParts.summary: Argues for fiscal resumption and a grant to restore royal finances.

contentParts.label: Chapter 15. How the king's council may be chosen and established
contentParts.summary: Proposes the constitution and operation of a royal council.

contentParts.label: Chapter 16. How the Romans prospered while they had a great council
contentParts.summary: Uses Roman precedent to support conciliar government.

contentParts.label: Chapter 17. Advice for giving the king's offices
contentParts.summary: Sets principles for appointments to royal office.

contentParts.label: Chapter 18. Advice on how corrodies and pensions may best be given
contentParts.summary: Regulates royal grants of corrodies and pensions.

contentParts.label: Chapter 19. How great good will grow from firmly endowing the Crown
contentParts.summary: Returns to the benefits of stable Crown endowment.

contentParts.label: Chapter 20. Advice for making patents of gifts
contentParts.summary: Concludes with rules for royal letters patent and gifts.

assertion.notes: The current BL record dates the Add MS 48031 A target to 1471-1476, and Plummer's chronological table gives the same range. Exact composition and revision sequence remains debated.

notes: Plummer argues that references to this land place the surviving form after Fortescue's return to England; the latest notice he identifies is 1476.

extent.description: Twenty numbered prose chapters represented by twenty BODY DIV1 elements, twenty HEAD elements, and twenty-one BODY P blocks. The target spans printed pp. 109-157 and fifty BODY PB milestones, including one leading blank before p. 109.

formStatements.description: Continuous expository political and legal prose arranged in twenty chapters.

genres.label: Advice to princes and governance counsel

languageStatements.label: Latin
notes: Modern English in Plummer's title page and paratext belongs to the source edition, not the medieval work's primary language.

scope.description: Brief Latin legal terminology and quoted matter embedded across the work

notes: The date 1471-1476 is a supported range for composition or revision of the surviving work, not a claim that every passage was first drafted within that interval.
Plummer's modern punctuation, capitalization, selected variants, and textual supply belong to the 1885 representation; they are not silently projected onto the abstract work.
Middle English is the work's primary language. Brief Latin terminology and quotations are recorded separately; modern English edition paratext is excluded from the work-language summary.
Content parts model the stable twenty-chapter intellectual structure. The XML's omitted half-title/front/back DIV1 scaffolding and retained title page are not counted as work sections.
The paired lineage manifest holds the full ten-witness graph, access status, cached facsimiles, and rights layers.

Direct inspection of the encoded chapters confirms substantive embedded Latin clauses and quotations, not merely Latin in the carrier title or chapter labels; Latin is therefore retained as a quoted work language while Middle English remains primary.

next_steps: Compare the principal recensions and current Fortescue scholarship.
Retain 1471-1476 as the supported range for the surviving work while distinguishing hypotheses about earlier formulation.
openQuestions.question: What is the exact composition, revision, and intended-recipient sequence between Fortescue's Lancastrian exile context and the extant 1471-1476 form associated with Edward IV?

next_steps: Collate Chapter XVIII endings and codicological transitions before changing the twenty-chapter abstract-work model.
openQuestions.question: Do the eighteen-chapter endings in Lambeth MS 262 and Digby 198 represent an earlier recension, deliberate abbreviation, or shared loss?

next_steps: Use the institutional request routes recorded in the lineage manifest.
Request whole manuscripts where feasible and record exact target folios inside each delivered object.
openQuestions.question: Can complete authorized facsimiles of the nine Plummer witnesses other than Digby 145 be obtained and mapped to their target portions?

assertion.notes: Plummer's internal argument supports England after 1471; no narrower place is established.

notes: Included to expose the editor named in the corpus source and viewer summary while keeping the work/edition boundary explicit.

scope.description: The 1885 source-edition representation only; not authorship of the abstract work

summaries.value: Fortescue contrasts absolute regal dominion with England's political-and-regal monarchy, then develops practical proposals for stable Crown revenue, protection of a prosperous commons, restraint of over-mighty subjects, a standing royal council, responsible officeholding, and disciplined royal grants.

Twenty numbered prose chapters: Chapters I-III establish constitutional distinctions; IV-XIV examine French and English finance, poverty, taxation, resumption, and Crown endowment; XV-XX address the royal council, appointments, pensions, and patents of gift.

The U-M XML verbally represents the carrier title page and Plummer's work text on printed pages 109-157. Its 412 PB milestones map the whole scanned carrier, but omitted front and back matter are not transcribed content and the retained title page is not an intrinsic chapter.

notes: Plummer cautions that this title most exactly describes only the first, speculative part of the work.
titles.value: The Difference between an Absolute and a Limited Monarchy

titles.value: Sir John Fortescue on the Governance of England

notes: Recorded as scholarly shorthand, not asserted as the medieval author's original title.

work.author: Sir John Fortescue

publication.embeddedTitle: The governance of England: otherwise called The difference between an absolute and a limited monarchy. A rev. text edited with introd., notes, and appendices by Charles Plummer.

work.summary: Fortescue contrasts absolute regal dominion with England's political-and-regal monarchy, then develops practical proposals for stable Crown revenue, protection of a prosperous commons, restraint of over-mighty subjects, a standing royal council, responsible officeholding, and disciplined royal grants.
